_comment: Authored from Document 8 Section 3 (Diverge Content Commissioning Workflow). Being authored step-by-step against the corpus; steps not yet reached remain PLACEHOLDER.
_phase_convention: phase is a required numeric field on WorkflowStepDefinition but is not read by any runtime logic or UI (verified: only appears in the type definition and this JSON) — it is a presentation-only sort/grouping key, not a corpus assertion. Values: 0 = Sprint Planning + Phase 1 Prerequisite Checks (Document 8 §3.2–§3.3, steps that precede the corpus's own numbered phases); 1 = Phase 1: Generate (§3.4); 2 = Phase 2: Review (§3.5); 3 = Phase 3: Approve (§3.6); 4 = Phase 4: Publish/Sync (§3.7); 5 = Sprint Closure (§3.10, after the corpus's numbered phases). 1–4 match Document 8 §3.1's own phase names exactly; 0 and 5 are out-of-band sort keys for steps the corpus does not number, not corpus-defined phase numbers.

steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead queries the Sanity operational dashboard for current coverage_status per (solution_category, role, buying_stage) tuple across all active solution categories, and identifies tuples below the next activation threshold (Document 4 §7.4 escalation pipeline output if an alert has fired; otherwise a direct Sanity gap query).
steps.validation_rule: At least one (solution_category, role, buying_stage) tuple below threshold must be identified before the sprint can proceed to node-list assembly (Step 2). A query returning no gaps is a valid outcome — it means no sprint is currently warranted.
steps.why_this_matters: This is the entry point to the entire Diverge Content Commissioning workflow. The coverage gap query determines which (solution_category, role, buying_stage) tuples are under-served, which is what the rest of the 12-step sprint exists to fix. Skipping or rushing this step risks commissioning content for tuples that aren't actually the highest-priority gaps.
steps.deferral_consequence: Not applicable — this step has no defer control. Without running the coverage gap query, there is no sprint scope to plan against; Step 2 (Node List Lock) has nothing to lock.
steps._note: No HOLD/FLAG triggers: Document 8 §3.2 describes this as a query/identification action with no pass/fail gate or escalation condition of its own — the corpus's gates appear later (§3.3 Phase 1 Prerequisite Checks). category: C and render_deferral_control: false because this is a practitioner-executed procedural action, not a client-data-attribute input with a deferral path; there is no corpus-described 'skip this and accept a named consequence' scenario for running a query.

steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead prioritizes gap tuples per Document 4 §7.5 (customer_engagement, employee_experience = High priority; risk_compliance, ai_platform = Medium), identifies missing module types per tuple (hero, benefits, cta, problem_framing, narrative module slot, proof, use_cases, outcomes, trust_signals, gated_assets, progressive_disclosure where applicable), assembles the full (solution_category, role, buying_stage, module_type) node list, and confirms that list with the Platform Engineer.
steps.validation_rule: The node list must be confirmed with the Platform Engineer before any generation work begins. Once confirmed, the node list is the sprint scope contract — no node outside the list is commissioned during the sprint without an explicit scope change.
steps.why_this_matters: The node list lock is what gives the sprint a bounded, accountable scope. Document 8 §3.2 step 5 calls this list "the sprint scope contract" — without it, commissioning work has no defined boundary, and Sprint Closure (Step 12) has nothing concrete to verify against. The Platform Engineer also needs advance notice of sprint volume to prepare the sync pipeline for approval-time load (§3.2 step 6).

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if advancement toward Phase 1 Prerequisite Checks (Step 3) is attempted before the node list has been confirmed with the Platform Engineer. Document 8 §3.2 step 6 requires this confirmation explicitly before generation work begins.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if advancement toward Phase 1 Prerequisite Checks (Step 3) is attempted before the node list has been confirmed with the Platform Engineer. Document 8 §3.2 step 6 requires this confirmation explicitly before generation work begins.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. Without a locked, Platform-Engineer-confirmed node list, there is no bounded sprint scope — Phase 1 Prerequisite Checks and all subsequent steps have no defined target tuples to operate against.
steps._note: category: A (not the Commissioning default of C) because Document 8 §3.2 step 6 makes Platform Engineer confirmation an explicit precondition before generation begins — this is a real corpus-described blocking requirement, not a procedural default. NOT INSTALLED: this step's H-01 is authored into hold_triggers_pending, not the live hold_triggers array. EVALUATOR DEFECT — flag-hold-evaluator.ts keys HOLD/FLAG codes globally by step_id with no workflow scoping (WorkflowStepDefinition has no workflow_id field). Commissioning step_id 2 collides with Onboarding step_id 2 (tal_member) — evaluateH01's only branches are step_id===10/17; step_id 2 falls through to a bare isNullish(submission.value) check, which has no corpus relationship to 'has the Platform Engineer confirmed the node list.' Populating the live hold_triggers array would let this wrong logic fire silently. Required fix before going live: extend the evaluator to dispatch on (workflow_id, step_id), which requires adding workflow_id to WorkflowStepDefinition — a code/schema change, out of scope for this authoring session. No FLAG is described for this step in the corpus.

steps.step_title: Phase 1 Prerequisite — Gate 1 Narrative Node Check
steps.corpus_authority: Document 8 §3.3 Gate 1; Gate 0 Delay note (§3.3 preamble)
steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead queries Sanity for an approved Narrative node matching the target (solution_category, buying_stage) pair: _type == "narrative" && solution_category == [target] && buying_stage == [target] && status == "approved".
steps.validation_rule: An approved Narrative node must exist for the target (solution_category, buying_stage) pair before Content Module generation (Step 7) can proceed. A node in status: under_review does not satisfy this gate — only status: approved satisfies it.
steps.why_this_matters: The Narrative node is the through-line source for every Content Module commissioned for its (solution_category, buying_stage) pair — its solution_claim, message_pillar, and supporting_claims are what later review stages (R1, R2) check Content Module claims against. Commissioning a module before its governing Narrative is approved risks generating content with no approved claim source to substantiate against, and for a new solution category this gate carries a 2–3 week lead time (Gate 0 delay note) that sprint planning must account for.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if no approved Narrative node exists for the target (solution_category, buying_stage) pair. Next action: route to Narrative node commissioning (Document 4 §8.3.1) — author and approve the missing Narrative node before Content Module generation proceeds.

Fires if the governing Narrative node exists but is in status: under_review. Document 8 §3.3 names this an explicit BLOCKING CONDITION. Next action: wait for the in-flight Narrative review to complete (or escalate the review if stalled) — do not commission against it and do not author a duplicate Narrative node. Commissioning for this (solution_category, buying_stage) pair remains blocked until the Narrative node returns to status: approved.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if no approved Narrative node exists for the target (solution_category, buying_stage) pair. Next action: route to Narrative node commissioning (Document 4 §8.3.1) — author and approve the missing Narrative node before Content Module generation proceeds.

Fires if the governing Narrative node exists but is in status: under_review. Document 8 §3.3 names this an explicit BLOCKING CONDITION. Next action: wait for the in-flight Narrative review to complete (or escalate the review if stalled) — do not commission against it and do not author a duplicate Narrative node. Commissioning for this (solution_category, buying_stage) pair remains blocked until the Narrative node returns to status: approved.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. Content Module generation for this (solution_category, buying_stage) pair cannot proceed without an approved Narrative node — Gate 1 is the first of four ordered prerequisite gates, and gates below a failed gate are not evaluated (§3.3 preamble).
steps._note: category: A and dual HOLD codes because Document 8 §3.3 names this gate's failure mode an explicit BLOCKING CONDITION with a defined routing outcome — directive, precondition-bearing language per the Step 2 precedent. H-01 and H-02 are split (not unified) because they describe distinct failure states with divergent remedies: H-01's remedy is to commission a new Narrative node; H-02's remedy is to wait for or escalate an existing in-flight review — authoring a new node in the H-02 case would be wrong. NOT INSTALLED: authored into hold_triggers_pending, not the live hold_triggers array. EVALUATOR DEFECT — flag-hold-evaluator.ts keys HOLD codes globally by step_id with no workflow scoping. Commissioning step_id 3 collides with Onboarding step_id 3 (tal_account_type_source, a mapping-table field) — evaluateH01/evaluateH02's only special cases are step_id===10/17; step_id 3 falls through to generic null-check / mapping-table-membership logic that has no relationship to Narrative-node status. Populating the live array would let this wrong logic fire silently. Required fix before going live: extend the evaluator to dispatch on (workflow_id, step_id) — a code/schema change, out of scope for this authoring session. No FLAG is described for this gate in the corpus.

steps.step_title: Phase 1 Prerequisite — Gate 2 Audience Node Check
steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead confirms an approved Audience node exists for the target (solution_category, role) pair.
steps.validation_rule: An approved Audience node must exist for the target (solution_category, role) pair before Content Module generation (Step 7) can proceed. Audience nodes are human-authored, not Compose-generated, and typically take one to two days per solution category to author for all five roles.
steps.why_this_matters: The Audience node is the authoring reference Kalder Compose and content authors use to produce role-specific content for a solution category — its content_preferences field is a generation parameter Compose reads when drafting. Without an approved Audience node for the target role, Content Module generation has no role-authoring guidance to draw from for that solution category.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if no approved Audience node exists for the target (solution_category, role) pair. Next action: route to Audience node authoring (human-authored, Document 4 §8.3) before Content Module generation proceeds.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if no approved Audience node exists for the target (solution_category, role) pair. Next action: route to Audience node authoring (human-authored, Document 4 §8.3) before Content Module generation proceeds.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. Content Module generation for this (solution_category, role) pair cannot proceed without an approved Audience node — Gate 2 is the second of four ordered prerequisite gates, evaluated after Gate 1 passes.
steps._note: Single HOLD code (unlike Gate 1's two-code split) because the corpus describes only one failure state for this gate: Audience node absent. Neither Document 8 §3.3 nor Document 4 §8.2/§1.2 describes an under_review branch for Audience nodes — the Audience node schema (Document 4 §1.2) does not even carry a status field, only coverage_status. category: A because this gate blocks Content Module generation with the same force as Gate 1. NOT INSTALLED: authored into hold_triggers_pending, not the live hold_triggers array. EVALUATOR DEFECT — flag-hold-evaluator.ts keys HOLD codes globally by step_id with no workflow scoping. Commissioning step_id 4 collides with Onboarding step_id 4 (tal_program_status, a mapping-table field) — evaluateH01's only special cases are step_id===10/17; step_id 4 falls through to a bare isNullish(submission.value) check with no relationship to Audience-node existence. Required fix before going live: extend the evaluator to dispatch on (workflow_id, step_id) — a code/schema change, out of scope for this authoring session. No FLAG is described for this gate in the corpus.

steps.step_title: Phase 1 Prerequisite — Gate 3 JTBD Node Check
steps.corpus_authority: Document 8 §3.3 Gate 3; Document 4 §6 intended_axes (module-type jtbd_ref requirement, verified directly: cta, gated_assets, proof, use_cases are Required; hero, benefits, narrative, problem_framing, outcomes, trust_signals, progressive_disclosure are Optional); Document 4 §8.6 Function 2 (GROQ cross-document validation, forward reference)
steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead first determines whether this gate applies: jtbd_ref is required only for Content Module nodes of module types with buying_job in intended_axes — cta, gated_assets, proof, and use_cases (Document 4 §6). If the target module type is not one of these four, Gate 3 does not apply and the sprint proceeds to Gate 4. If it does apply, the Content Ops Lead confirms an approved JTBD node exists for the target jtbd_code and solution_category, and the Platform Engineer confirms the JTBD node's solution_category field matches the target Content Module's solution_category.
steps.validation_rule: Gate 3 applies only to cta, gated_assets, proof, and use_cases module types. For those module types, an approved JTBD node must exist for the target jtbd_code and solution_category, and its solution_category field must match the target Content Module's solution_category exactly — this is the same cross-document integrity check GROQ validation Function 2 enforces at approval time (Step 10), checked here pre-emptively to avoid a late-stage approval failure.
steps.why_this_matters: For the four buying_job-bearing module types, the JTBD node is what content generation targets for task-specific framing — generating without it (or against a mismatched solution_category) produces a draft that will fail GROQ validation Function 2 at approval (Document 8 §3.6), wasting the full Generate-Review cycle. For the other seven module types, this gate is correctly inapplicable — applying it anyway would block sprint progress with no corpus basis.

hold_triggers.condition: Applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases. Fires if no approved JTBD node exists for the target jtbd_code and solution_category. Next action: route to JTBD node commissioning before Content Module generation proceeds.

Applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases. Fires if an approved JTBD node exists but its solution_category field does not match the target Content Module's solution_category. Next action: correct the jtbd_ref to point at the JTBD node with the matching solution_category, or commission a new JTBD node for the correct solution_category — do not proceed with the mismatched reference, as it will fail GROQ validation Function 2 at approval (Step 10).

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases. Fires if no approved JTBD node exists for the target jtbd_code and solution_category. Next action: route to JTBD node commissioning before Content Module generation proceeds.

Applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases. Fires if an approved JTBD node exists but its solution_category field does not match the target Content Module's solution_category. Next action: correct the jtbd_ref to point at the JTBD node with the matching solution_category, or commission a new JTBD node for the correct solution_category — do not proceed with the mismatched reference, as it will fail GROQ validation Function 2 at approval (Step 10).

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred for applicable module types (cta, gated_assets, proof, use_cases) — Content Module generation for these module types cannot proceed without a matching approved JTBD node. Not applicable (and therefore not a deferral question) for the other seven module types.
steps._note: CONDITIONAL GATE — applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases (Document 4 §6), unlike Gates 1–2 which are universal. The conditionality is encoded in the HOLD conditions' applicability clause, not a separate schema field — WorkflowStepDefinition has no applicability/conditional-gate primitive. NOT INSTALLED: authored into hold_triggers_pending, not the live hold_triggers array. EVALUATOR DEFECT (confirmed by reading lib/guided/flag-hold-evaluator.ts and lib/guided/state-machine.ts): the evaluator keys HOLD/FLAG codes globally by step_id with NO workflow scoping — WorkflowStepDefinition has no workflow_id field, and findStep()/evaluateStep() are only ever given a single resolved step object with a bare step_id integer, no indication of which workflow it came from. evaluateH01/evaluateH02 hardcode their real logic by Onboarding step_id (step_id===10, ===17) and fall through to a generic null/boolean-type check for every other step_id, including Commissioning step_id 5 (which collides with Onboarding step_id 5, tal_region — a free-text string field with no relationship to JTBD/module-type/solution_category matching). If this step's live hold_triggers were populated, evaluateH01/H02 would silently apply Onboarding's wrong fallthrough logic. This is a hard defect, not a documentation gap: the prior P0-2 build-fix's empty hold_triggers: [] arrays on Commissioning/Monitoring were incidentally the only thing preventing this collision from manifesting, since an empty array means evaluateStep's loop never invokes the evaluator at all. Required fix before going live: extend the evaluator to dispatch on (workflow_id, step_id), which requires adding workflow_id to WorkflowStepDefinition — a code/schema change, out of scope for this authoring session. Two HOLD codes for the same divergent-remedy reason as Gate 1: H-01's remedy is commissioning a new JTBD node; H-02's remedy is correcting or replacing a reference.

steps.step_title: Phase 1 Prerequisite — Gate 4 Supporting Claims Seeding
steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead confirms the governing Narrative node's supporting_claims array contains at least 5 entries. If it has fewer than 5: invoke Kalder Compose in supporting_claims seeding mode (Compose generates a candidate pool of 8–12 role-differentiated supporting claims derived from the Narrative node's solution_claim and message_pillar), review the candidate pool, curate it to a minimum of 5 entries, and update the Narrative node's supporting_claims array directly. This remedy happens within this step — it is not a referral to a separate commissioning path like Gates 1–3.
steps.validation_rule: The governing Narrative node's supporting_claims array must contain at least 5 entries before Content Module generation (Step 7) proceeds. Compose-generated candidates require Content Ops Lead curation before being added — an uncurated candidate pool does not satisfy this gate.
steps.why_this_matters: supporting_claims is the secondary claim pool Content Module generation is restricted to drawing from (Document 8 §3.4 step 2) — Compose is instructed not to introduce claims outside it. An array with fewer than 5 entries gives generation too narrow a pool to produce role-differentiated content without either repeating claims or drifting outside the approved through-line. This gate ensures the claim pool is wide enough before generation begins, rather than discovering the gap during R1 review (Step 8) after a draft already exists.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if the governing Narrative node's supporting_claims array contains fewer than 5 entries. Remedy is performed within this step: invoke Compose seeding mode, curate the generated candidate pool to at least 5 entries, and update the Narrative node's supporting_claims array. The gate clears once the array reaches 5 or more curated entries — there is no separate routing destination to wait on, unlike Gates 1–3.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if the governing Narrative node's supporting_claims array contains fewer than 5 entries. Remedy is performed within this step: invoke Compose seeding mode, curate the generated candidate pool to at least 5 entries, and update the Narrative node's supporting_claims array. The gate clears once the array reaches 5 or more curated entries — there is no separate routing destination to wait on, unlike Gates 1–3.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. Content Module generation cannot proceed with an insufficient supporting_claims pool — but unlike Gates 1–3, the remedy does not require waiting on a separately-tracked commissioning unit; it is a same-step seeding-and-curation procedure the Content Ops Lead can complete directly.
steps._note: TOPOLOGY VARIANT — this gate's remedy is a same-step self-resolving procedure (invoke Compose seeding mode → curate ≥5 → update the array), not a route-out-and-return like Gates 1–3 (which send the practitioner to a separate Narrative/Audience/JTBD commissioning path and block until that separate work completes and returns). The corpus prescribes the actual remedy procedure here, so it is authored into input_required as an executable sub-procedure rather than just a routing instruction. Structurally this is still a single binary HOLD (array has ≥5 entries or it doesn't) and fits the existing HOLD model without a schema change. NOT INSTALLED: authored into hold_triggers_pending, not the live hold_triggers array. EVALUATOR DEFECT — flag-hold-evaluator.ts keys HOLD codes globally by step_id with no workflow scoping (see Step 5's _note for the full mechanism). Commissioning step_id 6 collides with Onboarding step_id 6 (tal_marquee, a boolean field) — evaluateH01's only special cases are step_id===10/17; step_id 6 falls through to a bare isNullish(submission.value) check with no relationship to a supporting_claims array length. Required fix before going live: extend the evaluator to dispatch on (workflow_id, step_id) — a code/schema change, out of scope for this authoring session. category: A because Document 8 §3.3 lists this as one of the four ordered prerequisite gates with the same blocking force as Gates 1–3; no FLAG is described for this gate in the corpus.

steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead selects the target (solution_category, role, buying_stage, module_type) combination from the locked sprint node list, confirms the generation context Compose pre-populates (module_type, role, solution_category, buying_stage, jtbd_code if applicable, the governing Narrative node's solution_claim, message_pillar, and supporting_claims array), and invokes Compose. Compose outputs a draft content_body and populates module_type, role, solution_category, buying_stage, phase, narrative_ref, jtbd_ref (if applicable), label, and content_body on the Sanity draft record. Compose's output scope excludes confidence_tier_minimum, proof_refs, and asset_refs — these are not in Compose's output set and are populated later, by human reviewer judgment in Phase 2 (Step 9).
steps.validation_rule: If jtbd_code is applicable for this module type (per Gate 3's cta/gated_assets/proof/use_cases list), it must be populated in the generation context before invoking Compose. Saving the node sets status: draft and enters it into the review queue — this is the only condition that gates the save itself.
steps.why_this_matters: An absent jtbd_code for a module type that requires jtbd_ref produces a draft that will fail GROQ validation Function 2 at approval time (Step 10) — Document 8 §3.4 step 3 names this consequence explicitly. Catching it here avoids a wasted Generate-Review cycle, the same rationale Gate 3 pre-empts at the prerequisite stage.

hold_triggers.condition: Applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases (jtbd_ref required per Gate 3). Fires if jtbd_code is not populated in the generation context before Compose is invoked. Next action: populate jtbd_code from the Gate 3-confirmed JTBD node before invoking Compose; do not generate and fix afterward.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Applies only when the target module type is cta, gated_assets, proof, or use_cases (jtbd_ref required per Gate 3). Fires if jtbd_code is not populated in the generation context before Compose is invoked. Next action: populate jtbd_code from the Gate 3-confirmed JTBD node before invoking Compose; do not generate and fix afterward.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. Generation cannot proceed without the assembled context — this step produces the draft that Phase 2 Review operates on.
steps._note: CONTENT CORRECTION applied during review: Document 8 §3.4 step 5 says Compose 'must not populate' confidence_tier_minimum/proof_refs/asset_refs — a constraint on Compose's output behavior, not a validation condition on whether the saved draft is acceptable (the corpus never says the draft is rejected if these are populated; it explicitly assigns them to Phase 2 human judgment). Authored as an input_required output-scope fact, not a validation_rule gate. CONDITIONAL CATEGORY: category A is conditional on module type here, same as the gate itself — for the seven module types where jtbd_ref is not required, this step has no blocking precondition and category A's 'blocking force' does not actually apply. Noted as a seam between the flat category field and the conditional gate it's paired with, consistent with Gate 3's same conditionality. Lighter-touch step otherwise: descriptive generation procedure (Step 1 precedent) with one corpus-named precondition carrying an explicit failure consequence (Step 2/3 precedent: directive, consequence-bearing language → HOLD); conditional applicability mirrors Gate 3's shape exactly, no new topology. NOT INSTALLED, same evaluator defect as Steps 2–6 (step_id 7 collides with Onboarding step_id 7, tal_channel — an enum field; falls through to evaluateH01's generic null check, unrelated to jtbd_code presence). No FLAG described in the corpus for this step.

steps.corpus_authority: Document 8 §3.5 (Stage R1 factual accuracy, R2 through-line, R4 brand voice; legal review branch for proof_type: customer_reference)
steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead applies the review track appropriate to the node's module_type (long-form: R1 → R2 → R4 in sequence; short-form for cta/problem_framing: combined R2/R4 pass — R1 does not apply to short-form). R1: confirm every quantitative or substantiating claim in content_body traces to the Narrative node's solution_claim, message_pillar, supporting_claims, or a linked Proof node. R2: confirm no claim is introduced beyond the Narrative node's approved through-line, and that a Champion-module sentence and an Economic-Buyer-module sentence for the same (solution_category, buying_stage) would be mutually compatible if each described Kalder's core capability in one sentence. R4: standard editorial check — grammar, tone, brand vocabulary. If a Proof node with proof_type: customer_reference is required, apply the legal review branch: the module proceeds through the full review sequence to status: approved with that proof_ref omitted, and is updated with a targeted R3-only re-review once legal approves the Proof node.
steps.validation_rule: A node failing any applicable stage (R1, R2, or R4) returns to status: draft with the failure reason recorded in the review log before the reviewer advances to the next queued node. Passing one stage does not imply another has been applied — R4 may fail after R1/R2 pass, and vice versa. Short-form modules (cta, problem_framing) skip R1 entirely and apply a combined R2/R4 pass instead of separate passes.
steps.why_this_matters: R1 and R2 are what keep every Content Module's claims traceable to its governing Narrative node — without them, role-differentiated content could drift into unsubstantiated or through-line-incompatible claims that undermine the buying group's ability to receive a consistent message across roles. The legal review branch (customer_reference Proof nodes) lets approval proceed without blocking the entire module on legal's timeline, while still gating the specific proof linkage behind legal sign-off.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if the content_body contains a claim that cannot be substantiated by the Narrative node's solution_claim, message_pillar, supporting_claims, or a linked Proof node (R1 failure — long-form track only). Next action: return to status: draft; the reviewer flags the specific claim and required substantiation source in the review log.

Fires if the content_body introduces a claim not derivable from the Narrative node's approved through-line, or fails the one-sentence Champion/Economic-Buyer compatibility test (R2 failure, both tracks). Next action: return to status: draft; the unapproved claim is either removed or escalated to the Content Ops Lead as a candidate addition to supporting_claims.

Fires if the content_body fails brand voice review — grammar, tone, or brand vocabulary (R4 failure, both tracks; combined with R2 for short-form). Next action: return to status: draft for editorial correction. Does not imply R1 or R2 also failed.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if the content_body contains a claim that cannot be substantiated by the Narrative node's solution_claim, message_pillar, supporting_claims, or a linked Proof node (R1 failure — long-form track only). Next action: return to status: draft; the reviewer flags the specific claim and required substantiation source in the review log.

Fires if the content_body introduces a claim not derivable from the Narrative node's approved through-line, or fails the one-sentence Champion/Economic-Buyer compatibility test (R2 failure, both tracks). Next action: return to status: draft; the unapproved claim is either removed or escalated to the Content Ops Lead as a candidate addition to supporting_claims.

Fires if the content_body fails brand voice review — grammar, tone, or brand vocabulary (R4 failure, both tracks; combined with R2 for short-form). Next action: return to status: draft for editorial correction. Does not imply R1 or R2 also failed.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. A node cannot advance toward Step 9 (confidence_tier_minimum assignment / R3) without passing its track's applicable review stages — review failures route back to status: draft, not forward.
steps._note: STRUCTURAL DECISION: Document 8 §3.5 describes R1/R2/R3/R4 as four stages under one Phase 2: Review section, with R3 (tagging completeness, including confidence_tier_minimum assignment) as a single stage with three internal tasks. The existing 12-step workflow splits Phase 2 into Step 8 (Editorial Pass) and Step 9 (confidence_tier_minimum Assignment) — this authoring treats Step 8 as R1+R2+R4 and the legal review branch, and Step 9 as R3's three tasks. This split is defensible (R3 task 2 carries the corpus's only explicit ENFORCEMENT callout and named escalation path) but is a structural choice, not a corpus-given division. Three HOLD codes (H-01/H-02/H-03) map 1:1 to R1/R2/R4 because each is described as an independently-failing stage with the same remedy shape (return to draft) but a distinct failure description and review-log entry — split on distinguishable failure description per the locked precedent. The legal review branch is authored as a described procedure in input_required rather than a HOLD, since the corpus explicitly says the module proceeds to status: approved rather than being held. NOT INSTALLED, same evaluator defect (step_id 8 collides with Onboarding step_id 8, tal_new_logo_eligible — boolean field, validation_type: None confirmed). H-01/H-02 generalization holds: mis-fires-wrong-condition (evaluateH01 → isNullish) / fails-closed-spurious (evaluateH02 → boolean-type fallback, near-always-true for non-boolean payload). H-03 is FAILS-OPEN: evaluateH03's switch has no step_id===8 branch and falls through to an unconditional return false — this code can never fire for this step regardless of input, the most severe dead-gate case found so far, distinct from H-02's most-severe live-blocking case at Step 5.

steps.corpus_authority: Document 8 §3.5 Stage R3 (Tagging completeness review, tasks 1–3); Document 4 §3.3 confidence_tier_minimum (tagging standard, allowed values, fallback level mapping, validation behavior)
steps.input_required: Reviewer performs three tasks: (1) Confirm Compose-populated tag fields are correct — role, solution_category, buying_stage, jtbd_code (if applicable), phase. (2) Assign confidence_tier_minimum: HIGH (all three indicators — direct role-address language, a role-specific quantified outcome claim, a role-assumptive CTA — must be met simultaneously), MEDIUM (role-appropriate framing without role-exclusive claims; the expected tag for most v1 modules), LOW or UNKNOWN (cross-role appropriate, solution-category framing, or explicit fallback-level content). (3) Populate proof_refs and asset_refs if applicable, linking substantiating Proof and Asset nodes.
steps.validation_rule: confidence_tier_minimum must be one of HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/UNKNOWN (§3 CONFIDENCE_TIERS) — null is not a valid exit state from this step. Task 1's tag fields must match Compose's populated values exactly or the node returns to status: draft. Task 3 is conditional — proof_refs/asset_refs are populated only where applicable; their absence is not itself a failure unless a required substantiating link is missing.
steps.why_this_matters: confidence_tier_minimum is read directly by Adobe Target at session time to gate offer eligibility — a HIGH-tagged module is served only to Level 1 visitors, while MEDIUM reaches Levels 1–2, LOW reaches Levels 1–3, and UNKNOWN serves all levels. This is a live runtime gate, not a Sanity bookkeeping field: an over-tagged HIGH module (one that only meets MEDIUM criteria) silently under-serves MEDIUM-confidence visitors who could have benefited from it — a coverage gap that looks like a personalization success in the data (no error, just absence) and is therefore hard to detect after the fact. Document 4 explicitly names this over-tagging risk and instructs reviewers to challenge HIGH tags that don't meet all three indicators simultaneously.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if confidence_tier_minimum is null or not one of HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/UNKNOWN at attempted advancement to status: approved. Document 8 §3.5 names this an explicit ENFORCEMENT condition: 'A node may not advance to status: approved with a null confidence_tier_minimum field.' Next action: the reviewer assigns one of the four values using the decision rule; if uncertain which value applies, escalate to the Content Ops Lead for determination — this is the corpus's named default escalation path, distinct from Gates 1/3's wait-for-an-external-process remedy. This is task-2-scoped only; it does not gate tasks 1 or 3.

Fires if any Compose-populated tag field (role, solution_category, buying_stage, jtbd_code if applicable, phase) is found incorrect during task 1 review. Next action: return to status: draft for correction. This is task-1-scoped.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if confidence_tier_minimum is null or not one of HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/UNKNOWN at attempted advancement to status: approved. Document 8 §3.5 names this an explicit ENFORCEMENT condition: 'A node may not advance to status: approved with a null confidence_tier_minimum field.' Next action: the reviewer assigns one of the four values using the decision rule; if uncertain which value applies, escalate to the Content Ops Lead for determination — this is the corpus's named default escalation path, distinct from Gates 1/3's wait-for-an-external-process remedy. This is task-2-scoped only; it does not gate tasks 1 or 3.

Fires if any Compose-populated tag field (role, solution_category, buying_stage, jtbd_code if applicable, phase) is found incorrect during task 1 review. Next action: return to status: draft for correction. This is task-1-scoped.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. A node cannot advance to Step 10 (Approve) without a valid confidence_tier_minimum — Document 4 names this Blocking at Sanity publication independent of this workflow's own gating.
steps._note: TASK-SCOPED GATING, not step-wide: Document 8 §3.5's ENFORCEMENT callout is positioned directly under R3 task 2 (confidence_tier_minimum), before task 3 begins — it is scoped to task 2 only, not the full R3 stage (confirmed by corpus blockquote placement, not inferred). Task 1 has its own remedy (return to draft) but no enforcement callout or escalation path; task 3 is conditional ('if applicable') with no enforcement language at all, so no HOLD is authored for task 3. Two HOLD codes because tasks 1 and 2 have distinguishable failure descriptions and task 2's remedy carries a named escalation path task 1 doesn't have. ESCALATION VARIANT, not a new topology: Gate 1's H-02 escalation is 'wait for an already-in-flight external review to complete'; this step's H-01 escalation is 'the reviewer is uncertain and hands the decision to the Content Ops Lead' — a judgment-overflow escalation, not a wait-for-another-process gate. Both are remedy-text variants within the same single-binary HOLD topology, not structurally distinct gate shapes — the topology (universal / conditional / in-step-remedy / task-scoped) is the level that requires new schema treatment; the remedy description (route-out, wait-and-escalate, judgment-overflow-escalate, self-resolve) is documentation-only variation within a topology and does not. RUNTIME SIGNIFICANCE: confidence_tier_minimum is the first Commissioning field authored so far with a live, named EXTERNAL runtime consumer — Adobe Target reads it directly at session time (Document 5, multiple sections: hero/benefits/cta/gated_assets/proof offer gating) to determine visitor eligibility. This is categorically different from workflow-internal-only fields (e.g. phase, confirmed unused at runtime) or fields whose only consumer is this workflow's own dormant gates. NOT INSTALLED, same evaluator defect: step_id 9 collides with Onboarding step_id 9 (tal_open_pipeline, validation_type: None, confirmed). H-01 generalization holds (mis-fires-wrong-condition: evaluateH01 → isNullish(value) — coincidentally close to 'is the field null' but still wrong, since it can't validate enum membership against HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/UNKNOWN, and a non-null garbage value would wrongly pass). H-02 generalization holds (fails-closed-spurious: validation_type None confirmed → evaluateH02 boolean-type fallback, near-always-true for the non-boolean tag-correctness payload). EVALUATOR FIX REQUIREMENT 4 — task-level granularity: this step's two HOLDs are scoped to different sub-tasks (task 1 vs. task 2) within one step, but StepSubmission carries one undifferentiated value field per step with no sub-task addressing. Dispatch fix (workflow_id, step_id) and reachable-branch fix do not address this — a step with task-scoped HOLDs cannot be correctly evaluated by a one-value-per-step submission shape even after both prior fixes land. This is the first requirement that touches submission STRUCTURE, not just evaluation logic, and is a different kind of change from requirements 1–3.

steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead advances the node from status: under_review to status: approved in Sanity. This action triggers three automated GROQ cross-document validation functions — no additional practitioner input is required beyond initiating the transition; the validation is automated, not a judgment call.
steps.validation_rule: All three functions must pass for the transition to complete: (1) the referenced Narrative node's status must be approved; (2) if jtbd_ref is present, the referenced JTBD node's solution_category must match the Content Module's solution_category; (3) the referenced Narrative node's solution_category and buying_stage must both match the Content Module's corresponding fields. If any function fails, the transition is blocked and the node returns to status: under_review.
steps.why_this_matters: These three functions are the structural enforcement of the cross-document integrity this workflow has been building toward since Gates 1 and 3 (Steps 3, 5) — they confirm at approval time that the relationships established earlier in the sprint are still valid. A node could pass review (Steps 8–9) on its content quality while its Narrative or JTBD references have drifted out of sync (e.g. the Narrative was retired or amended mid-sprint) — these functions catch that drift before the node reaches the offer catalog.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if Function 1 fails: the referenced Narrative document's status is not approved. Sanity error: "Narrative node [id] is not in status: approved. Content Module approval blocked." Next action: resolve the Narrative node's status (it may have entered under_review or been retired since Step 3's Gate 1 check) before re-attempting approval.

Applies only when jtbd_ref is present on the node. Fires if Function 2 fails: the referenced JTBD document's solution_category does not match the Content Module's solution_category. Sanity error: "JTBD node [id] solution_category [value] does not match Content Module solution_category [value]. Content Module approval blocked." Next action: correct the jtbd_ref — this is the same cross-document integrity check Step 5's Gate 3 H-02 pre-empted; a Function 2 failure at this step means Gate 3's check was passed but the reference drifted afterward, or Gate 3 was bypassed.

Fires if Function 3 fails: the referenced Narrative document's solution_category or buying_stage does not match the Content Module's corresponding fields. Sanity error: "Narrative node [id] scope mismatch on [field]. Content Module approval blocked." Next action: correct the narrative_ref or the Content Module's own solution_category/buying_stage field, whichever is actually wrong.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if Function 1 fails: the referenced Narrative document's status is not approved. Sanity error: "Narrative node [id] is not in status: approved. Content Module approval blocked." Next action: resolve the Narrative node's status (it may have entered under_review or been retired since Step 3's Gate 1 check) before re-attempting approval.

Applies only when jtbd_ref is present on the node. Fires if Function 2 fails: the referenced JTBD document's solution_category does not match the Content Module's solution_category. Sanity error: "JTBD node [id] solution_category [value] does not match Content Module solution_category [value]. Content Module approval blocked." Next action: correct the jtbd_ref — this is the same cross-document integrity check Step 5's Gate 3 H-02 pre-empted; a Function 2 failure at this step means Gate 3's check was passed but the reference drifted afterward, or Gate 3 was bypassed.

Fires if Function 3 fails: the referenced Narrative document's solution_category or buying_stage does not match the Content Module's corresponding fields. Sanity error: "Narrative node [id] scope mismatch on [field]. Content Module approval blocked." Next action: correct the narrative_ref or the Content Module's own solution_category/buying_stage field, whichever is actually wrong.

steps.deferral_consequence: Cannot be deferred. A node cannot reach status: approved without passing all three functions — Step 11 (Publish/Sync) only fires on the approved transition event, so there is nothing to defer forward to.
steps._note: CLEANEST TOPOLOGY SO FAR: three independently-named functions, each corpus-given an exact, quotable error string. Function 2's jtbd_ref-present conditionality mirrors Gate 3's (Step 5) applicability test exactly, not a new conditional shape. AUTOMATION DISTINCTION (new, real): unlike every practitioner-judgment step so far (R1–R4 review, confidence_tier_minimum assignment), this gate is Sanity-automated — the Content Ops Lead's only action is initiating the transition; the three GROQ functions execute without human judgment. This means these workflow-level HOLDs are a MIRROR of Sanity's own enforcement, not an independent check this workflow's evaluator is responsible for originating — Sanity blocks the transition regardless of whether this workflow's evaluator ever runs. Whether these HOLDs are load-bearing post-evaluator-fix is therefore an open design question for the fix session: they may only need to exist for UI/audit-log visibility (surfacing Sanity's already-enforced block to the practitioner), not as a second independent enforcement layer — flagged for the close-out. D8-Flag-05 (GROQ query load-testing under 10 concurrent approvals, Platform Engineer responsibility) is a pre-launch platform readiness task, not a per-node content gate, so it is named in corpus_authority but not authored as a HOLD/FLAG on this step — routed to the close-out's outstanding-before-production list rather than dropped. NOT INSTALLED, evaluator defect: step_id 10 collides with Onboarding step_id 10 (sfdc_opportunity_created/sfdc_opportunity_stage, validation_type: mapping_table). H-01 GENERALIZATION BREAKS HERE (verified, not assumed): Onboarding step_id 10 has its OWN special-cased branch in evaluateH01 ('fires when sfdc_opportunity_created is true but mappingTableProvided !== true') rather than the generic isNullish fallthrough used at steps 2–9 — still mis-fires-wrong-condition as a category, but the specific wrong condition is a compound boolean+mapping-table-presence check, not a bare null-check; a test plan must assert against THIS specific condition, not the steps-2–9 shape. H-02 falls through to evaluateH02's mapping_table branch — with no allowed_values defined on this Commissioning step, membership against an empty array is always false-for-inclusion, so this is fails-closed-spurious (same category as Step 3's H-02, different from the boolean-fallback variant at Steps 5/8/9). H-03 has no step_id===10 branch in evaluateH03 → FAILS-OPEN, same as Step 8's H-03.

steps.corpus_authority: Document 8 §3.7 (Publish/Sync — offer catalog write, phase: converge pre-write exclusion check, coverage status recomputation)
steps.input_required: No Content Ops Lead input is required at this step. On the status: approved event from Step 10, the sync pipeline (Platform Engineer-owned, Sanity-triggered) automatically writes module_type, role, solution_category, buying_stage, phase, confidence_tier_minimum, jtbd_code (derived), and fallback_level (derived) to the Adobe Target offer catalog; evaluates the phase: converge pre-write exclusion check before any write; and triggers coverage status recomputation for the affected (solution_category, role, buying_stage) tuple, rolling up to the solution-category-level coverage_status and writing it to the solution_category_coverage_status AEP attribute.
steps.validation_rule: Not applicable as a practitioner-facing validation — this step has no human-gated pass/fail condition for diverge nodes. The pipeline's own correctness is governed by the phase: converge pre-write exclusion check (synchronous, single-point-enforced per D8-Flag-04) and is verified retrospectively via the weekly catalog integrity audit and at Sprint Closure (Step 12), not at this step.
steps.why_this_matters: This is the step where commissioned content actually becomes servable — the offer catalog write is what makes a node visible to Adobe Target at session time. The phase: converge exclusion check is what prevents Champion-distribution-only content from leaking into the public-facing serving path; Document 8 names this a single-point enforcement specifically because no Target activity rule duplicates it, which is also why the weekly catalog integrity audit (a separate operational practice, not part of this workflow) exists as a backstop.
steps._note: SCOPE FINDING: Document 8 §3.7 assigns this entire phase to 'Platform Engineer (pipeline implementation and integrity monitoring); Sanity (event emission)' — no Content Ops Lead action is named for diverge nodes at trigger time. The only practitioner confirmation action this corpus describes for a phase: converge exclusion log entry appears in §4 (Converge workflow, the Content Ops Lead confirms the exclusion log entry is present for each newly approved converge node), not in §3.7 itself, and this 12-step workflow's scope is diverge nodes only (§3.1). Authoring a practitioner confirmation gate here by analogy to §4 would not be corpus-grounded for this workflow's actual scope — declined deliberately. category: C and render_deferral_control: false because there is no practitioner action to defer — this step describes automated pipeline behavior for the practitioner's awareness, matching Step 1's precedent for procedural steps with no corpus-described gate. NO PENDING GATE FIELDS (unlike Steps 2–10): this is a genuinely different outcome from the dormant-gate convention — there is no gate logic to design at all here, not gate logic that exists but isn't installed. The weekly catalog integrity audit (Platform Engineer, recurring operational practice) and Step 12 Sprint Closure (which verifies tuple-level coverage and exclusion-log entries for the whole sprint) are where this phase's correctness actually gets checked — not here. PRODUCTION DEPENDENCY: D8-Flag-04's single-point exclusion enforcement has no redundant check — the weekly catalog integrity audit is its SOLE backstop. This audit is a load-bearing external practice this step's integrity story depends on entirely; routed to the close-out's platform-level production-correctness dependencies list alongside D8-Flag-05.

steps.corpus_authority: Document 8 §3.10 (five closure conditions; Sprint Closure Checklist)
steps.input_required: Content Ops Lead confirms: (a) every node in the sprint node list is either status: approved or, if not, carried forward to the next sprint with its failure reason documented; (b) the solution_category_coverage_status AEP attribute reflects the updated coverage state for all solution categories affected by this sprint. Platform Engineer confirms: (c) the operational dashboard shows correct tuple-level coverage for all (solution_category, role, buying_stage) pairs commissioned in the sprint; (d) the pending_solution_fallback event rate for advanced solution categories begins declining within 48 hours of the sprint's final approval events; (e) any phase: converge exclusion log entries produced during the sprint are reviewed and confirmed as expected (from a concurrent Section 4 converge workflow) rather than unexpected. The Content Ops Lead and Platform Engineer jointly sign off only once all five conditions are confirmed.
steps.validation_rule: All five conditions must be confirmed before the sprint is closed. Condition (a) is satisfied either by full approval or by documented carry-forward — it is not a 100%-approval requirement. Conditions (b) through (e) are verification conditions with no documented-exception path; each must affirmatively match its expected post-sprint state.
steps.why_this_matters: Sprint Closure is the only point in this workflow that verifies the sprint actually achieved what Step 2's node-list lock committed to — without it, a sprint could silently under-deliver (nodes stuck in draft, coverage attributes stale, fallback events not actually declining) while appearing complete. Condition (d) in particular closes the loop back to Step 1's original coverage gap query: if the fallback event rate isn't declining, the commissioned content didn't actually fix the gap it was commissioned to fix.

hold_triggers.condition: Fires if the solution_category_coverage_status AEP attribute does not reflect the expected post-sprint state for any solution category affected by this sprint (condition b). Next action: Content Ops Lead investigates before closure — this may indicate the sync pipeline (Step 11) did not propagate correctly.
Fires if the operational dashboard's tuple-level coverage does not match the sprint's actual deliverables (condition c). Next action: Platform Engineer investigates the discrepancy before closure.

Fires if the pending_solution_fallback event rate for an advanced solution category has not begun declining within 48 hours of the sprint's final approval events (condition d). Next action: Platform Engineer investigates whether the sync pipeline correctly processed all approval events and whether the AEP attribute update propagated to Target. This is the outcome-verification condition that closes the loop back to Step 1's original coverage gap query.

Fires if any phase: converge exclusion log entry produced during the sprint is unexpected — i.e., not attributable to a concurrent Section 4 converge workflow (condition e). Next action: investigate before the sprint is closed; an unexpected exclusion may indicate a diverge node was mistagged phase: converge.

flag_triggers.condition: Fires if any node in the sprint node list did not reach status: approved (condition a) but is being carried forward with a documented failure reason. This is non-blocking per the corpus's own carry-forward provision — closure may proceed once the failure reason is documented.
flag_triggers.consequence: Node is carried forward to the next sprint; its failure reason is recorded for that sprint's planning.

hold_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if the solution_category_coverage_status AEP attribute does not reflect the expected post-sprint state for any solution category affected by this sprint (condition b). Next action: Content Ops Lead investigates before closure — this may indicate the sync pipeline (Step 11) did not propagate correctly.
Fires if the operational dashboard's tuple-level coverage does not match the sprint's actual deliverables (condition c). Next action: Platform Engineer investigates the discrepancy before closure.

Fires if the pending_solution_fallback event rate for an advanced solution category has not begun declining within 48 hours of the sprint's final approval events (condition d). Next action: Platform Engineer investigates whether the sync pipeline correctly processed all approval events and whether the AEP attribute update propagated to Target. This is the outcome-verification condition that closes the loop back to Step 1's original coverage gap query.

Fires if any phase: converge exclusion log entry produced during the sprint is unexpected — i.e., not attributable to a concurrent Section 4 converge workflow (condition e). Next action: investigate before the sprint is closed; an unexpected exclusion may indicate a diverge node was mistagged phase: converge.

flag_triggers_pending.condition: Fires if any node in the sprint node list did not reach status: approved (condition a) but is being carried forward with a documented failure reason. This is non-blocking per the corpus's own carry-forward provision — closure may proceed once the failure reason is documented.
flag_triggers_pending.consequence: Node is carried forward to the next sprint; its failure reason is recorded for that sprint's planning.

steps._note: FIVE-CONDITION TOPOLOGY, closest to Step 10's three-function shape, with one corpus-described severity difference: condition (a) carries an explicit documented-exception path ('carried forward... with its failure reason documented'), read as satisfied-by-documentation rather than satisfied-by-100%-approval — authored as F-01 (FLAG, non-blocking), the FIRST FLAG authored in Commissioning. Conditions (b)-(e) have no such exception language and are authored as HOLDs (H-01 through H-04) — the first step combining both HOLD and FLAG codes for a corpus-described severity distinction within a single step, completing the gate model found across this workflow: TOPOLOGY (universal / conditional / in-step-remedy / task-scoped / no-gate) crossed with CODE TYPE (HOLD hard-block / FLAG non-blocking-but-tracked) crossed with GRANULARITY (step-wide / task-scoped), with remedy description (route-out, wait-and-escalate, judgment-overflow-escalate, self-resolve, investigate-and-correct) as prose-level variation within each cell, not a structural axis. Four HOLD codes for (b)-(e) split on distinguishable failure description plus distinct responsible practitioner (Content Ops Lead for b; Platform Engineer for c/d/e), matching the divergent-remedy/distinguishable-owner principle. NOT INSTALLED, evaluator defect — THIRD DISTINCT COLLISION SHAPE (verified): step_id 12 collides with Onboarding step_id 12 (Track 2 signal consent status, attribute: null), which has its OWN step_id===12 special cases in BOTH evaluateH03 (claimsTrack2Complete === true && dpaDocumentationConfirmed !== true) and evaluateF04 (claimsTrack2Complete !== true) — unlike steps 2-9 (generic fallthrough) or step 10 (a different single-function special case), step 12 applies Track-2 DPA/consent-classification logic to sprint-closure coverage/exclusion-log verification, a complete domain mismatch reinforcing the architectural severity of this defect. This step's own H-01/H-02/H-03/H-04 don't share codes with Onboarding's H-03 in a way that collides identically, but if this step's H-03 (pending_solution_fallback decline check) were ever evaluated, it would run Onboarding's Track-2 DPA logic instead — confirmed wrong. F-01 VERIFIED FAILS-OPEN: evaluateF01's branches are step_id===2/4/18 only; step_id 12 matches none, falls through to the function's bare final `return false` — confirmed by reading the function body, not asserted. This establishes that FLAG codes share the same three-mode failure-mode taxonomy as HOLD codes (fails-open / fails-closed-spurious / mis-fires-wrong-condition), so evaluator fix requirement 2 (every evaluate-function needs a reachable, semantically-grounded branch) applies equally to evaluateF-functions, not just evaluateH-functions. TWO CLOSE-OUT THREADS: condition (e) depends on a concurrent Section 4 converge workflow this pass did not author — its 'expected vs. unexpected' exclusion judgment has an external dependency this workflow's authored content cannot fully resolve alone. The carry-forward provision (condition a) is the corpus's own realistic acknowledgment that not every sprint fully closes its gaps — worth carrying into the close-out as the corpus's intended normal-operation behavior, not a degraded-mode exception.
